checks.a_analysis: The output content was carefully compared against the source. Descriptions of images such as 'Scan QR code to Ablr contact page' and 'Ablr logo' are appropriate AI-generated additions for accessibility. No completely invented paragraphs or sections were identified in the provided text.

Contact information on page 60 (Web: www.ablr360.com, Call: 833-294-2257, Email: kim.casey@ablr360.com) matches the source exactly. External URLs like artsy.net on page 18 also match the original document text.

The output document is missing the vast majority of the source material. A literal placeholder in the JSON sections array confirms that 51 sections (representing over 80% of the 60-page document) have been omitted. Missing content includes 'Think Accessibility' (p. 5), 'Access by Disability Type' (p. 6), 'Keyboard Access' (p. 7), and dozens of slides between pages 21 and 58.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF contains 60 pages of content including sections on 'Keyboard Access', 'Screen Reader Super Powers', 'MS Word Checklist', etc.

checks.a_analysis: On page 8, the source PDF displays a complex set of data tables (Schedules D, E, F, G, H, and I) containing over 20 rows of data. The output identifies a table element but only provides a single row containing a truncated title fragment, failing to extract any of the actual data rows from the form image.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 8 form image containing tables for Schedules D through I with multiple data rows.
checks.b_evidence_output: A single table row with content: "Magnification – A Di"

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: On page 18 ('Links Practice'), the source document lists four 'Before' examples of bad link text (e.g., '1) Click here for more information...') and four 'After' examples. The output entirely omits the instructional 'Before' text, which constitutes 50% of the textual information on that slide.
checks.b_evidence_source: 1) Click here for more information on related events; 2) Applications are due by August 12th, and link to apply can be found here; 3) Visit Artsy’s website at https://www.artsy.net/; 4) You can learn more about Sally’s work on her website
checks.b_evidence_output: [The sentences listed above are absent from the JSON element]

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text extracted into the table on page 8 is truncated incorrectly. Instead of reflecting the slide content, it shows 'Magnification – A Di', which is a nonsensical fragment of the slide title 'Magnification – A Different View'. This wording change prevents a user from receiving accurate information.
checks.b_evidence_location: Magnification – A Different View, page 8, table element

checks.a_analysis: The output sections correspond to the pages of the source document. No unique text blocks from the source are repeated in the output more times than they appear in the original. While the company logo 'ablr' appears frequently, this is intentional repetition in the source design and is correctly preserved.

The heading hierarchy follows a logical flow. Section titles are generally treated as H1/H2, and subsections (e.g., Section 8 'User-friendly design') use H2. No skips in heading levels (e.g., H1 to H3) were observed in the provided segments.

The data table on page 20 ('Choosing Fonts: Serif vs. Sans Serif') is accurately represented in Section 21 of the JSON. It correctly identifies the header row and aligns the font examples into the appropriate columns as seen in the source.

The reading order in the JSON matches the visual flow of the PDF. For example, on page 60, the contact information precedes the 'Thank You!' message and the contact QR code, which is faithfully reflected in Section 61.

Lists are correctly identified and categorized. The bullet points on page 8 are converted to an unordered list in Section 8, and the numbered instructions on page 59 are correctly converted to an ordered list in Section 60.

checks.check_name: Heading count mismatch between JSON and HTML
checks.a_analysis: There is a significant discrepancy between the number of headings in the semantic.json (17) and the rendered HTML (75). This exceeds the allowable 10% or 3+ heading threshold, indicating that the renderer is adding a large number of headings not present in the structured data.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source document contains numerous headings across its 60 pages, consistent with the metadata count of 74.
checks.b_evidence_output: Headings in semantic.json: 17; Headings in rendered HTML: 75

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains several interactive links, particularly on the 'Resources' page (p. 44) and the 'Get in touch' page (p. 60). The JSON only records 2 links, while the rendered HTML shows 20. This indicates a failure to properly extract and structure all valid links from the source into the semantic JSON, with many links being treated as plain text (e.g., in Section 61).
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 44 contains 5 distinct resource URLs. Page 60 contains a website and an email address link.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output faithfully translates the slide-based content of the source PDF into a continuous vertical layout. A Table of Contents has been added at the top for navigation, which is an expected enhancement. The horizontal layout of text and images on individual slides (e.g., Slide 2 team photos, Slide 7 keyboard photos, Slide 35 MS Word screenshots) has been linearized into a logical vertical flow in the HTML, preserving the intended meaning and relationship of content. The visual organization of information remains consistent, allowing a user to find the same content in the same relative sequence as the source.

Data tables present in the source PDF (Slides 29, 30, and 31) are rendered correctly as HTML tables. They include visible borders, clear row/column separation, and proper alignment of data. For example, the 'UAL Units (Attachment C)' table from Slide 29 and the 'Consent Agenda (Attachment D)' table from Slide 30 are fully readable and maintain their tabular structure in the HTML rendering. The 'Trails' table on Slide 27, which is part of an integrated map graphic in the source, is also clearly visible in the HTML output.

All meaningful images from the source PDF are present and correctly rendered in the HTML output. This includes logos (Ablr logo), team photographs (Slide 2), illustrative icons (Slide 6 disability types, Slide 10 superhero), complex screenshots (Slide 19 PDF tags, Slide 23 color tools, Slide 27 maps), and the QR code on the final slide (Slide 60). No broken image icons are visible, and meaningful diagrams (like the SmartArt workaround on Slide 36) have been successfully converted into images.

There is no evidence of unintended content duplication caused by the conversion process. Content repeats only where it intentionally repeats in the source PDF, such as Slide 31 ('Determining Tables') which re-presents tables from previous slides for discussion. The HTML output follows the slide order exactly without adding or duplicating sections erroneously.

The overall visual quality of the HTML conversion is high. The text is sharp and readable, headers are consistently styled to define sections, and there is appropriate whitespace between elements. The layout is clean and professional, representing the original presentation's intent well within a web-based format. There are no overlapping elements, excessive gaps, or misaligned blocks of text.
